title: The Renaissance
summary: The cultural and intellectual movement that transformed European art, literature, philosophy, and science between roughly 1300 and 1600 -- centered in Italian city-states, organized through patronage, and driven by the recovery and celebration of ancient Greek and Roman learning.
hook: The Renaissance named itself. The scholars and artists of 15th-century Italy decided they were living through a rebirth -- a recovery of ancient greatness after centuries of decline. This act of self-naming was itself a historical innovation: a civilization consciously breaking with its recent past and identifying a more distant past as the model for its future. Whether the Renaissance actually was what it claimed to be is one of the most productive debates in intellectual history. What is not in doubt is that it produced some of the most enduring art, architecture, and thought in Western history.

beginner.theMovement: The Renaissance was a cultural and intellectual movement that transformed European civilization between roughly 1300 and 1600. It began in the city-states of Italy and spread across Europe. Its defining feature was the recovery and celebration of ancient Greek and Roman culture -- literature, philosophy, art, and architecture. Renaissance thinkers believed that the ancient world represented a peak of human achievement. They wanted to recover it and apply it to their own time. The name itself -- Renaissance means rebirth in French -- captures this self-understanding. The movement produced some of the most celebrated art, architecture, and literature in Western history.
beginner.origins: The Renaissance grew from several converging conditions. The Italian city-states were wealthy from trade and banking. This wealth created patrons who could commission art and scholarship. Italy had access to Byzantine scholars fleeing the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453. These scholars brought Greek manuscripts and learning. The Black Death of the 1340s had disrupted traditional religious authority and created new urgency about human life and its meaning. And Italy's ruins provided a constant visible reminder of ancient greatness. These conditions came together nowhere else in Europe at the same time.
beginner.coreCommitments: The Renaissance organized itself through patronage. The Medici family in Florence, the Este in Ferrara, and the papacy in Rome competed to attract scholars and artists. Humanist scholars developed new methods of textual criticism. The printing press, arriving in Europe in the 1450s, transformed the spread of Renaissance ideas. It made texts available at a cost and scale that manuscript copying could never achieve. Ideas that had circulated among scholars in one city could now reach educated readers across Europe.
beginner.whatItChanged: The Renaissance produced extraordinary achievements. In art, Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael, and Botticelli developed new techniques -- perspective, anatomical accuracy, chiaroscuro -- that transformed painting and sculpture. In architecture, Brunelleschi's dome for Florence Cathedral revived classical forms. In literature, Petrarch, Boccaccio, and Machiavelli transformed prose and poetry. In political thought, Machiavelli's The Prince established the analysis of politics as an independent discipline. In science, the Renaissance created conditions for the Scientific Revolution that followed.
beginner.legacyAndLimits: The Renaissance's legacy is contested. The traditional view, articulated by Jacob Burckhardt in 1860, presented it as the birth of modernity -- the moment when individual selfhood and secular values replaced medieval religious conformity. This narrative has been substantially revised. Medieval historians have shown that earlier periods had their own intellectual vitality. Feminist historians have shown that the Renaissance's celebration of the individual largely excluded women. The period was genuinely transformative. But the claim that it invented modernity is more contested than Burckhardt believed.
beginner.whyItEnded: The Renaissance as a distinct movement gave way to two developments. The Protestant Reformation, beginning in 1517, transformed the religious context in which Renaissance culture had flourished. Protestant iconoclasm disrupted patronage systems in northern Europe. The Scientific Revolution -- which the Renaissance had made possible -- eventually moved beyond Renaissance frameworks. By the early 17th century, the Renaissance had been absorbed into broader European intellectual culture rather than persisting as a distinct movement.

general.theMovement: Jacob Burckhardt's The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy (1860) established the framework within which the Renaissance has been understood ever since. Burckhardt argued that the Renaissance represented the birth of modern individualism: the Italian city-state created conditions in which people could understand themselves as individuals rather than as members of a collective. This individualism expressed itself in the arts, in political life, in the cultivation of the self as a project. Almost everything subsequent historians have argued about the Renaissance has been in dialogue with this framework -- either extending it, qualifying it, or rejecting it.
general.origins: The relationship between the Renaissance and the Byzantine East has been substantially revised by scholarship on the Council of Florence (1438-1439) and the fall of Constantinople (1453). The Council brought Greek Orthodox clergy and scholars to Italy for reunion negotiations -- creating sustained intellectual contact between Latin and Greek intellectual traditions at exactly the moment when Italian humanism was developing its interest in Greek philosophy. George Gemistos Plethon's advocacy for Platonic philosophy at the Council directly inspired Cosimo de' Medici's foundation of the Platonic Academy in Florence. The Byzantine transmission was not incidental to the Renaissance but constitutive of some of its most important intellectual developments.
general.coreCommitments: Lisa Jardine's work on Renaissance patronage has shown how the culture of learned display -- the demonstration of classical learning as a form of social performance -- shaped what the Renaissance produced. Patrons did not simply commission what they wanted; they competed in the cultivation of scholars, artists, and the cultural products they produced. The humanist scholar's ability to read and write classical Latin and Greek was a form of social capital in the courts and cities of Renaissance Italy. This culture of learned display had specific effects: it privileged particular types of classical knowledge, required that learning be translated into recognizable cultural forms, and created hierarchies among the practitioners of Renaissance culture.
general.whatItChanged: Niccolo Machiavelli represents perhaps the clearest case of the Renaissance's genuine intellectual innovation. Political philosophy had existed before Machiavelli. But his claim that politics should be analyzed as it actually is rather than as it should be -- that the successful prince must be willing to use force, fraud, and cruelty when necessary, regardless of conventional morality -- represented a genuine break with the tradition that had preceded him. The Prince (1513) is the founding text of modern political realism. Its influence on subsequent political thought -- from Hobbes to Gramsci to Henry Kissinger -- is direct and traceable.
general.legacyAndLimits: Joan Kelly's influential 1977 essay asked: 'Did Women Have a Renaissance?' Her answer was that they did not -- that the cultural and economic conditions that enabled the Renaissance's achievements for elite men actively constrained women. The artistic and literary culture of the Renaissance celebrated the active public life of the citizen-scholar; women were excluded from the public sphere that this culture required. Kelly's argument has been refined and debated but its core insight -- that historical periodization is gendered -- has been fundamental to subsequent feminist historiography.
general.whyItEnded: The relationship between the Renaissance and the Reformation is complex and paradoxical. Erasmus -- the greatest humanist scholar of the Northern Renaissance -- was sympathetic to Luther's critique of church corruption but horrified by the Reformation's consequences for intellectual culture. The humanist program of textual criticism had prepared the ground for Reformation biblical scholarship; the Reformation's iconoclastic destruction of art and its suspicion of human learning were antithetical to everything the humanist program stood for. The Reformation divided the European republic of letters along confessional lines in ways that permanently altered the conditions under which Renaissance culture had flourished.

advanced.theMovement: The debate about the Renaissance's relationship to the medieval period has been the most productive organizing question in the historiography. Burckhardt's sharp break has been progressively qualified. Charles Haskins demonstrated in 1927 that the 12th century had its own renaissance; subsequent historians of medieval philosophy, science, and art have shown the genuine intellectual vitality of those centuries. The question is not whether the Renaissance represented a genuine transformation -- it clearly did -- but whether that transformation was as radical as Burckhardt claimed, and whether the periodization obscures significant continuities with what preceded it.
advanced.origins: Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve (2011) -- a Pulitzer Prize-winning popular history arguing that Poggio Bracciolini's rediscovery of Lucretius's De Rerum Natura in 1417 was a pivotal event in the transformation of Western thought -- represents the popular reception of Renaissance humanist philology at its most successful and most contested. Historians of classical reception have criticized Greenblatt's account for overstating the impact of a single text and for reproducing a Whig history of modernity that the historiography has substantially moved beyond. The broader debate about the role of specific textual recoveries versus longer-term institutional and intellectual developments in shaping the Renaissance is substantively unresolved.
advanced.coreCommitments: The gender of patronage in the Renaissance has received increasing attention. Women patrons -- Isabella d'Este, Eleonora of Aragon, Margaret of Austria, Vittoria Colonna -- played significant roles in Renaissance cultural production that earlier scholarship systematically ignored. Cecilia Berti's and Stephen Campbell's work on women in Italian Renaissance courts has shown that women's patronage operated under different constraints and through different cultural forms than men's -- producing different relationships between patron and artist and different distributions of cultural prestige. This scholarship does not reverse Kelly's argument about women's exclusion but complicates it: exclusion from some cultural forms coexisted with participation in others.
advanced.whatItChanged: The Renaissance's relationship to the Scientific Revolution remains one of the most debated questions in the historiography of early modern science. The traditional narrative treats the Renaissance as the precondition for the Scientific Revolution: the recovery of ancient science, the development of naturalistic art, and the culture of empirical observation created the intellectual tools that Galileo and Newton deployed. This narrative has been complicated by work showing that much of what the Renaissance recovered from antiquity was wrong -- Galenic medicine, Ptolemaic astronomy -- and had to be overcome rather than extended. The relationship is better understood as complex: the Renaissance created certain preconditions while also establishing orthodoxies that the Scientific Revolution had to challenge.
advanced.legacyAndLimits: The contemporary relevance of the Renaissance has been most influentially argued by Stephen Greenblatt, whose Shakespeare's Negotiations and Renaissance Self-Fashioning developed the New Historicism approach -- reading literary texts against their broader cultural contexts and emphasizing the construction of identity as a historical process. Greenblatt's approach has been enormously influential in literary studies while attracting the standard criticisms: that it tends to read historical context in ways that serve contemporary theoretical interests rather than historical understanding. The broader question of whether the Renaissance provides a productive model for contemporary humanist practice -- or whether invoking it reproduces a Eurocentric and gendered cultural hierarchy -- remains actively debated.
advanced.whyItEnded: The translation of Renaissance culture across national contexts -- to France, England, Spain, and the German-speaking lands -- raises fundamental questions about what the Renaissance designates as a historical phenomenon. Each national context received and transformed Italian Renaissance culture differently, producing distinctive national traditions that share family resemblances without being identical. Whether these national traditions should be understood as developments or dilutions of the Italian Renaissance, or as independent cultural formations that happened to draw on similar sources, shapes how the Renaissance is periodized and what it is understood to have accomplished.

foundation.theStory: The Renaissance -- meaning rebirth -- was a cultural and intellectual movement in which European scholars and artists of the 14th to 16th centuries recovered and redeployed the learning of ancient Greece and Rome. Beginning in the wealthy city-states of Italy and spreading across Europe, it produced transformations in art, architecture, literature, political thought, and natural philosophy that are still considered foundational to Western culture. But it also generated significant historical debates about who it included, what it actually represented, and whether it was as revolutionary as it claimed to be.
foundation.theDebate: The central debate concerns Jacob Burckhardt's argument that the Renaissance represents the birth of modern individualism and secular culture. Joan Kelly's feminist challenge -- 'Did women have a Renaissance?' -- reveals that this narrative of progress is gendered: what represented liberation for elite men represented exclusion or regression for women. The broader question is whether historical periodization can be assessed without asking 'progress for whom?'
foundation.whyItMatters: The Renaissance matters as the immediate precondition for the Scientific Revolution, the Reformation, and the European expansion that produced the modern world. It also matters as a case study in how cultural movements work: how patronage shapes cultural production, how the recovery of the past can serve present needs, and how a movement's self-understanding shapes but does not determine its historical significance.
questions: Jacob Burckhardt argued that the Renaissance represented the birth of modern individualism. Joan Kelly argued that this claim applies only to elite men, not to women. How should historians handle periodizations that describe genuine transformations for some social groups while describing exclusion or regression for others?

The Renaissance is defined partly by its own self-understanding -- the scholars and artists of the period understood themselves as participating in a rebirth of ancient culture. How much should historians rely on a movement's self-understanding in defining what it was? What might a Renaissance historian see that a historian looking only at external evidence might miss -- or get wrong?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The historiography of the Renaissance is defined by the tension between Burckhardt's foundational framework and subsequent revisionist scholarship. Joan Kelly's feminist challenge, medievalist challenges (Haskins's Renaissance of the 12th century), and postcolonial challenges have all qualified Burckhardt's narrative without producing a consensus replacement. Peter Burke's The Italian Renaissance provides the most accessible synthesis of revisionist scholarship. Lisa Jardine's Worldly Goods presents the economic and material conditions of Renaissance culture.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The documentary record of the Renaissance is rich but unevenly distributed. The archives of Italian city-states, records of patronage relationships, and the correspondence of humanist scholars provide extensive evidence. Women's participation requires more careful archival work than the male-dominated canon. The experience of craftsmen, minor artists, and non-elite audiences is significantly less accessible. The relationship between Renaissance culture and the broader population -- how far humanism penetrated beyond elite circles -- is genuinely difficult to assess from surviving sources.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on the Renaissance must engage seriously with the question of who it included and excluded -- the Renaissance's celebration of individual achievement was explicitly gendered and class-specific. It must also engage with the question of what was genuinely new -- distinguishing real innovations from continuities with medieval culture that the Renaissance narrative obscured. And it must address the relationship between the Renaissance's self-understanding (as rebirth) and its historical reality (as transformation of a continuous tradition).
questions: Machiavelli's The Prince argued that political life should be analyzed as it actually is, not as moral philosophy says it should be. Is Machiavelli's realism a genuine analytical advance -- enabling more accurate understanding of politics -- or a moral regression -- legitimizing ruthlessness? Can these two assessments be reconciled?

Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve argued that the rediscovery of Lucretius's De Rerum Natura in 1417 was a pivotal event in the transformation of Western thought toward secularism and modernity. Historians have criticized this for overstating the impact of a single text. What methodological questions does this debate raise about how intellectual historians identify causation?

research.topic: The role of Byzantine scholars and Greek manuscripts in the Italian Renaissance
research.content: The Council of Florence (1438-1439) and the fall of Constantinople (1453) brought Greek scholars and manuscripts to Italy in ways that substantially enriched humanist learning. The recovery of Platonic philosophy -- transmitted through Byzantine scholars like George Gemistos Plethon -- directly inspired the Platonic Academy in Florence. The textual recovery of Greek science, philosophy, and literature provided the humanist movement with materials that the Latin tradition alone could not have supplied. This Byzantine dimension of the Renaissance origin story is now well established.

research.topic: Women's patronage and cultural production in the Renaissance
research.content: Scholarship on women's roles in Renaissance cultural production has substantially expanded in the past three decades, complicating Joan Kelly's influential argument. Isabella d'Este, Vittoria Colonna, and other elite women exercised significant cultural patronage; a smaller number of women artists and writers -- Sofonisba Anguissola, Gaspara Stampa, Veronica Franco -- achieved recognized cultural production in male-dominated fields. The emerging picture is of exclusion from some cultural forms alongside participation in others, under constraints that differed significantly from those facing male participants.

research.topic: The actual delivery and reception of humanist learning beyond elite circles
research.content: How far humanist learning actually penetrated beyond the educated elite in Renaissance Italy -- what the artisans, merchants, and working population of Florence or Venice knew of the classical revival and how it affected their lives -- cannot be fully recovered from surviving sources. What evidence would resolve this: systematic records of reading practices, school curricula, and vernacular literary production at the non-elite level. Why unavailable: literacy rates were low outside urban elites; non-elite reading and listening practices were not systematically recorded; archival survivorship strongly favors elite documentation. What can be responsibly inferred: that vernacular literary culture and visual culture (public art, civic ceremony) transmitted elements of humanist ideas to non-elite audiences. What must remain unknowable: the degree to which non-elite Florentines or Venetians understood their world to be transformed by the humanist program that their social superiors were consciously promoting.

comparativeNarrative.name: The City as Cultural Laboratory
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of the Italian city-states -- Florence especially -- the Renaissance was inseparable from the specific form of civic life that Italian urban republics and principalities had developed. The patronage of art and scholarship was not merely aesthetic but political: the Medici commissioned Botticelli and Michelangelo as part of a strategy of legitimating their power through cultural prestige. The humanists wrote political philosophy for real political situations. Machiavelli's Prince was written in the specific context of Medici power in Florence. Understanding the Renaissance requires understanding the city-states that generated it -- their wealth, their political instability, and their intense competition for status and power.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Renaissance That Excluded Women
comparativeNarrative.content: Joan Kelly's 1977 question -- 'Did Women Have a Renaissance?' -- established that the Renaissance's narrative of progress was gendered. The conditions that enabled Renaissance achievement for elite men -- participation in public civic life, access to universities and learned institutions, freedom to travel -- were precisely the conditions denied to women. The Renaissance's idealization of the active citizen-scholar and its celebration of ancient male literary and philosophical culture produced a cultural program from which women were structurally excluded. Subsequent scholarship has complicated Kelly's argument -- women did participate through patronage, correspondence, and some forms of artistic production -- but her core insight about gendered periodization remains foundational.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Reformation's Complicated Relationship
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of northern Europe, the Renaissance arrived filtered through the humanist movement of Erasmus and his contemporaries -- less focused on visual art, more focused on textual scholarship and religious reform. The overlap between humanist criticism of clerical corruption and Protestant reforming impulse was real, but the divergence was also real: Erasmus remained Catholic while Luther reformed. The Northern Renaissance cannot be separated from the Reformation that it partly enabled and that partly destroyed it -- the cultural patronage systems, the ecumenical scholarly networks, and the confidence in human learning that humanists had cultivated were all disrupted by confessional division.

rabbitHole.label: The Scientific Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Renaissance created the intellectual and institutional preconditions for the Scientific Revolution -- the recovery of ancient science, the culture of empirical observation, and the patronage systems that supported learned inquiry

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: The Reformation both drew on humanist textual scholarship and disrupted the patronage systems that had sustained the Renaissance -- the relationship between the two is complex and essential

rabbitHole.label: The Printing Press
rabbitHole.reason: The printing press transformed the reach and speed of Renaissance ideas -- making it possible for humanist learning to spread from Italian cities to educated readers across Europe

rabbitHole.label: Leonardo da Vinci
rabbitHole.reason: Leonardo represents the Renaissance ideal of the universal man -- artist, scientist, engineer, anatomist -- whose notebooks embody the movement's aspiration to unite humanistic learning and empirical investigation

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The same period that produced the Renaissance also produced European expansion -- the two are connected through the courts that patronized both humanist scholarship and exploratory voyages

rabbitHole.label: Petrarch -- Father of Humanism
rabbitHole.reason: Petrarch's recovery of Cicero's letters, his invention of the concept of the Dark Ages, and his creation of the sonnet form make him arguably the Renaissance's founding figure and deserving of dedicated treatment

rabbitHole.label: Machiavelli
rabbitHole.reason: Machiavelli's Prince and Discourses represent the Renaissance's most consequential contribution to political philosophy -- his separation of political analysis from moral philosophy is still the foundational move of modern political realism

rabbitHole.label: The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy -- Jacob Burckhardt (1860)
rabbitHole.reason: Burckhardt's foundational account -- still readable and still productive as the framework against which all subsequent revision has organized itself -- is the essential starting point for anyone engaging seriously with the period

reference.author: Jacob Burckhardt
reference.title: The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy
reference.annotation: Burckhardt's foundational account, written in German and published as Die Kultur der Renaissance in Italien, established the framework within which the Renaissance has been understood ever since -- and against which all subsequent revision has organized itself. Still worth reading as a genuinely illuminating account of the period's cultural character.

reference.author: Peter Burke
reference.title: The Italian Renaissance: Culture and Society in Italy
reference.annotation: The most accessible and balanced scholarly account of Renaissance culture and its social conditions -- revising Burckhardt while preserving what is valuable in his framework. Burke's attention to the social and institutional basis of cultural production complements the intellectual history tradition.

reference.author: Lisa Jardine
reference.title: Worldly Goods: A New History of the Renaissance
reference.annotation: Jardine's account of the Renaissance's material and commercial dimensions -- the trade in art, manuscripts, and luxury goods that underpinned humanist culture -- provides an essential corrective to accounts focused purely on ideas. The Renaissance as economic as well as intellectual phenomenon.

reference.author: Niccolo Machiavelli
reference.title: The Prince (Il Principe)
reference.annotation: Machiavelli's most famous work, written in Italian in 1513 and published posthumously in 1532, is the Renaissance's most consequential contribution to political philosophy -- establishing the analysis of politics as it actually is rather than as it should be. Essential primary source, available in multiple modern translations.

reference.author: Joan Kelly
reference.title: Women, History, and Theory
reference.annotation: Contains Kelly's foundational 1977 essay 'Did Women Have a Renaissance?' -- which established that the Renaissance's narrative of cultural progress was gendered. Essential for any serious engagement with the Renaissance's social dimensions.

reference.author: Stephen Greenblatt
reference.title: The Swerve: How the World Became Modern
reference.annotation: Greenblatt's Pulitzer Prize-winning account of the recovery of Lucretius's De Rerum Natura is the most influential recent popular history of Renaissance humanism -- accessible, eloquent, and contested by specialists who argue it overstates the impact of a single text.

commerce.title: The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy
commerce.author: Jacob Burckhardt
commerce.note: The foundational account -- still readable 160 years later and essential for understanding both the period and the historical debate it generated

commerce.title: Worldly Goods
commerce.author: Lisa Jardine
commerce.note: Jardine's material history of the Renaissance is the essential corrective to purely intellectual accounts -- the art market, the trade in manuscripts, and the economics of patronage

commerce.title: The Swerve
commerce.author: Stephen Greenblatt
commerce.note: The most accessible popular account of humanist textual recovery -- readable, provocative, and productively argued-with rather than simply accepted

commerce.title: The Prince
commerce.author: Niccolo Machiavelli
commerce.note: Short enough to read in an afternoon and inexhaustible in its implications -- the Renaissance's most important political text

commerce.title: The Medici: Godfathers of the Renaissance -- PBS (2004)
commerce.note: A documentary overview of the Medici family's patronage and its consequences for Florentine cultural life -- the best visual introduction to the institutional foundations of Renaissance art

popularCulture.title: Medici: Masters of Florence -- Netflix (2016-2019)
popularCulture.description: The Netflix series dramatizes the rise of the Medici banking dynasty and their role as patrons of Renaissance art and learning -- following Cosimo de' Medici, Lorenzo the Magnificent, and their descendants through the political crises and artistic achievements of 15th-century Florence. Substantially dramatized but capturing the basic truth that the Renaissance was built on banking profits and political ambition as much as humanist idealism.

popularCulture.title: The Birth of Venus -- Sandro Botticelli (c. 1484-1486)
popularCulture.description: Botticelli's painting -- commissioned by the Medici and showing the goddess Venus rising from the sea -- is one of the most reproduced images in Western art history. Its subject matter (a pagan goddess, nude) and its style (classical drapery, idealized figure) embody the humanist program of recovering ancient culture while transforming it into something distinctly Renaissance. The painting's enormous subsequent popularity has made it a visual shorthand for 'the Renaissance' in popular consciousness.

popularCulture.title: The Dome of Florence Cathedral -- Filippo Brunelleschi (1436)
popularCulture.description: Brunelleschi's dome for Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence -- built between 1420 and 1436, the largest dome constructed since the Pantheon in Rome -- is the Renaissance's most spectacular engineering achievement. No one in medieval Europe knew how to build a dome of this scale without scaffolding; Brunelleschi invented techniques that had not existed before. The dome still dominates the Florentine skyline and demonstrated that the Renaissance was not merely a literary movement but a transformation of the built world.

popularCulture.title: Leonardo da Vinci's notebooks
popularCulture.description: Leonardo's notebooks -- containing drawings, observations, and speculations on anatomy, engineering, optics, geology, and dozens of other subjects -- are the most extraordinary surviving artifacts of the Renaissance ideal of the universal man. They were not published in Leonardo's lifetime and most of their technical content had no influence on the subsequent development of science. But they have become the most reproduced visual documents of the Renaissance in popular culture -- evidence that one person could encompass the full range of human knowledge in a single lifetime.

popularCulture.title: The Agony and the Ecstasy -- Irving Stone (1961)
popularCulture.description: Stone's biographical novel about Michelangelo -- following him from his apprenticeship through the Sistine Chapel and the Pieta -- is one of the most widely read fictional accounts of a Renaissance artist's life. It conveys the physical labor of Renaissance art (the years of lying on scaffolding to paint the Sistine Chapel ceiling), the relationship between artist and patron, and the religious and civic dimensions of Renaissance artistic production with an emotional accessibility that art history rarely achieves.
